name: developer-tool-modern
summary: A technical-product homepage archetype that uses product-native proof, concise benefit copy, and a distinctive shell to make complex tooling feel credible and approachable.
goals: Earn trust quickly with real product surfaces and operational evidence.
Keep technical buyers engaged without falling into generic enterprise marketing.
Balance strong brand personality with clear task-oriented conversion paths.
layout_rules: Lead with one dominant value frame and put product proof immediately in or below the hero.
Use a recognizable shell such as a product canvas, faux desktop, or banded technical surface.
Alternate denser proof sections with calmer reset zones so the page does not feel like a wall of tooling.
typography_rules: Keep headlines short and technically legible.
Use one typographic move for brand personality, then keep the rest of the system disciplined.
Constrain line length aggressively in explanatory copy.
component_rules: Invest in one signature product-proof shell rather than many decorative card styles.
Use tabs, selectors, and evidence panels to explain product breadth without overloading one screen.
Treat the footer as a structured utility layer with docs, trust, and ecosystem links.
motion_rules: Keep motion stateful and functional: tab changes, panel swaps, gallery movement, and subtle reveals.
Avoid cinematic effects unless they reinforce atmosphere without hiding product truth.
Provide reduced-motion-safe handling for interactive modules.
copy_rules: Use plain-language benefits before deep technical detail.
Let the tone feel audience-native, but keep the core explanation direct.
Use CTA labels that describe the literal next step, such as Explore, Deploy, Install, or Get started.
accessibility_rules: Preserve semantic structure inside faux desktop or app-shell framing.
Check dark panel contrast carefully, especially muted metadata and status colors.
Ensure tabs, radios, menus, and galleries are keyboard reachable and clearly labeled.
performance_rules: Optimize screenshots, product shells, and scripts before adding more atmospheric chrome.
Treat canvas or WebGL as optional enhancement, not baseline requirement.
Avoid stacking too many heavy first-party scripts on already interactive pages.
anti_patterns: Copying a source brand's palette or atmosphere without the underlying product concept.
Using dense desktop chrome that collapses into unreadable mobile screenshots.
Adding fake technical UI without believable states or proof content.
Choosing decorative motion over clear product explanation.
notes: This archetype fits developer tools, infrastructure products, and technical SaaS with strong product surfaces.
Voice can vary from calm and cinematic to playful and docs-native, but product truth should stay central.
